Hey again! 

I mostly fixed this by **clearing the flutter image cache** each time I navigate to the page where the images are picked. This fix has allowed me and my clients to use the app without crashing for a month or so now. So I think this solution works, at least in my case. 


This is how I clear the cache:
```dart
imageCache.maximumSize = 0;
imageCache.clear();
```

Some other considerations:
1. Use the **dev tools memory tab** in profile mode, and search for memory leaks. I had multiple.
  1.2. If there are you will see the memory rise each time you perform the affected action
2. Update all your dependencies
3. Check android permissions
4. Try building an empty app, with image_picker and check if it still happens.
  4.1. If it does not happen you can determine it has something to do with other implementations. 
  4.2. If it does happen, you can be certain it has to do with the plugin itself.
5. If nothing works, consider reporting a more official issue.


Hope this helps, it did for me

@Abhijithsp we've had very similar reports. Our solution has been to bundle different camera modules into the app and let the user try different plugins via a settings screen. It's been needlessly troublesome - especially since the `camera` package forces 16:9 aspect ratio - #80393 #15953 and #45665. Essentially, we're letting our users choose between `image_picker` (unstable and correct images) or `camera` (more stable, but incorrect images)

This is not something that can be fixed in the plugin; it is not technically a bug, although I understand the frustration with the effect it has on the experience. The reason that there aren't any logs or crash reports is that the app isn't crashing, it's being terminated by the OS to free memory, because while the image picker activity is active the Flutter application is technically a background app, and thus subject to collection by the OS when there is memory pressure.

* For end users, the solution is to implement state restoration in your application, and to use [these instructions from the plugin README](https://pub.dev/packages/image_picker/versions/0.8.4+4#handling-mainactivity-destruction-on-android) to get the results of the image picker activity during restoration.
* For the `flutter run` workflow, I don't see a solution other than re-attaching to the restored application on completion.

The only way to avoid this would be to implement a custom, in-application image picker experience from scratch. That's out of scope of this plugin—but is something that interested members of the community could certainly build.

(The reason this is specific to Android is that on iOS the system model is extremely different; instead of providing a standard set of activities for operations like these, the OS provides components that are displayed within the application, so the application that's doing the image selection is in the foreground the entire time. If at some point Android provides a similar component, `image_picker` could switch to that to resolve this.)

Given that this is, within the constrains of the way Android activities work, not something we can change, I'm going to close this. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/93807 tracks improving the README to make this more clear.

@stuartmorgan - this is a most excellent insight! Thank you

Do you have any articles we can read about how Android chooses which apps to terminate in the background? ie: does it select those using the most memory? If so, it may mean that we may have an undetected memory leak.

> Do you have any articles we can read about how Android chooses which apps to terminate in the background? ie: does it select those using the most memory?

Android documentation discusses it at a very high level, but there is (likely by design) not a clearly described set of rules; it's up to the OS to decide when to terminate processes, and which ones.

Given that how recently used the process was is called out as one factor that's likely to be considered, it could very well be the case that on some devices the image picker is using so much memory that **all** other non-essential processes are killed.

## FFI plugin

Add support for `flutter create --template=plugin_ffi` and `flutter run` support for `ffiPlugin: true` in `pubspec.yaml`.

This addresses 2 issues:

1. Currently, the way to do FFI with Flutter is to use `flutter create --template=plugin`. However, this generates boilerplate for plugin registration and example code for method channels which go unused.
2. We've see dozens of issues with building, linking, and bundling native code. The FFI plugin template should be the sample that works for all platforms for users to reference or use as a starting point.

[Design doc].
[Internal design doc].

Some relevant issues:

* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/78819
* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/77790

Included in this PR:

* Building shared C source code as part of the native build for platforms Android, iOS, Linux desktop, MacOS desktop, and Windows desktop.
* Sample code doing a synchronous FFI call.
* Sample code doing a long running synchronous FFI call on a helper isolate.
* Use of `package:ffigen` to generate the bindings.

Not included in this PR:

* Bundling closed source libraries. (MacOS, iOS, and Android require copying to the right place. Linux and Windows require referencing from CMake files.)
* Building closed source libraries with the right compiler options (most notably rpath and include path).
* Support for `flutter test`. This requires a `bin/setup.dart` to invoke a native build outside `flutter run` or `flutter build`.
* Support for using the generated package as Dart standalone. This requires a `bin/setup.dart` to invoke a native build.
* Linking against `dart_api_dl.h` to be able to invoke functions from `dart_api.h`.
* Sample code doing an asynchronous callback through native ports form `dart_api.h`.
* Compatibility between the `plugin` and `plugin_ffi` templates. The example apps (`example/lib/main.dart`) are different. @stuartmorgan we could change the plugin template to also perform a `sum` operation asynchronously and make them compatible that way.

Test this PR locally without building Flutter by running the Flutter commands from source (replace platforms for your host OS). 

```sh
$ dart <PATH_TO_FLUTTER_CHECKOUT>/packages/flutter_tools/bin/flutter_tools.dart create -t plugin_ffi --platforms=macos,ios,android my_ffi_plugin
$ cd my_ffi_plugin/example/
$ dart <PATH_TO_FLUTTER_CHECKOUT>/packages/flutter_tools/bin/flutter_tools.dart run -d macos
```

@mit-mit can you give it a spin and let me know what you think.

- [ ] I added new tests to check the change I am making, or this PR is [test-exempt].
- [ ] All existing and new tests are passing.

I've started reviewing this, but before I continue -

1. It would be good to discuss this in an API review meeting.
2. Have we considered other ways to provide a template for this? For example, a git repository? I'm also under the impression (maybe @craiglabenz knows?) that there's some work on going to make templates more pluggable. Could we do that for this?

My main thrust with question 2 is I suspect that people will want to heavily customize these. For example, someone might want to use a special compiler or build system that this doesn't use, or someone will want to use some other language choice that they'll write additional bindings for. It'd be really cool to have this as a stock/recommended/ready to go template for common use cases, but it'd be cool as well to see other templates pop up that support additional paradigms.

Thanks for the comments!

> For example, someone might want to use a special compiler or build system that this doesn't use

People can do that in their own app. However, that will not work for reusable plugin packages on pub.dev. For reusable packages we rely on everyone having the same build tools (checked with `flutter doctor`). The benefit of having a predefined set of build tools is that the users of FFI plugin packages do not even have to know native code is involved and need to know nothing about native build tools. They could be ordinary Dart developers.

For prebuilt shared libraries, distributed in binary I agree. I noted in the PR description that I think a sample repo might be a better option than a template (and it is not part of this PR).

> For example, a git repository?

We need to land the `pubspec.yaml` support for bundling and building native code without the registry that method-channel plugins use.

Whether the template lives in the Flutter repo or not does not matter so much. However:

1. I think a template is better than a sample, renaming things in the boilerplate is notoriously hard.
2. How would people find that template if it's not part of `flutter create`?

> It'd be really cool to have this as a stock/recommended/ready to go template for common use cases, but it'd be cool as well to see other templates pop up that support additional paradigms.

I agree that having other templates pop up would be super cool. I'm interested in any work going in that direction.

> Could we do that for this?

@mit-mit what is your take on how we should prioritize this?

>>Could we do that for this?

> @mit-mit what is your take on how we should prioritize this?

I'd love to see plugable for Flutter templates in general, but I don't want to hold this work up behind that. When we do plugable templates, let's try and do it across all of them and not just for a single one.

> 1. It would be good to discuss this in an API review meeting.

+1 on this

> 2. Have we considered other ways to provide a template for this? For example, a git repository? I'm also under the impression (maybe @craiglabenz knows?) that there's some work on going to make templates more pluggable. Could we do that for this?

I'm planning on working on pluggable templates in Q1 2022. Don't have a design doc written yet, though.

> > For example, someone might want to use a special compiler or build system that this doesn't use
> 
> People can do that in their own app. However, that will not work for reusable plugin packages on pub.dev. For reusable packages we rely on everyone having the same build tools (checked with `flutter doctor`). The benefit of having a predefined set of build tools is that the users of FFI plugin packages do not even have to know native code is involved and need to know nothing about native build tools. They could be ordinary Dart developers.

Strong +1 on this. We do not want the ecosystem to become a wild west where every time you add a new package dependency you might have to install an obscure compiler or build system.

I would love if we could make this work using our future pluggable template system rather than adding 3 new types of templates to the tool (we only have 4 today, so this would almost double the number of built-in templates).

Can we make this template also include the steps to compile the C library to Wasm, and link to it from JS, for web plugins? It would be amazing if someone could create a plugin that supported both VMs and web.
Should the plugin include best practices for using ffigen?

> Can we make this template also include the steps to compile the C library to Wasm, and link to it from JS, for web plugins? It would be amazing if someone could create a plugin that supported both VMs and web.

That would require emscripten, which is yet another build system constraint to deal with. But yes, it should be possible.

> Can we make this template also include the steps to compile the C library to Wasm, and link to it from JS, for web plugins? It would be amazing if someone could create a plugin that supported both VMs and web.

As I think was brought up in the design review, this would be really cool, but would have to be a future project, as we would have to consider an official supported C -> WASM for Flutter toolchain, with associated doctor validators.

I'm a bit late to the party here, but the generically pluggable templates project is in its very early stages and probably shouldn't impact decision-making here. A day may come when various templates elsewhere get migrated over, but that's not imminent.

I've updated the PR to:

* Exclude `dart_api_dl.h` for now. I'll follow up with another PR.
* Include a sample with a helper isolate for longer running native code.
* A lot more comments and documentation.

I think this should be good to go once CI is passing from my POV.

I'm interested in following up on the issue(s) related to dart_api_dl.h.

I would like to make sure @blasten has a chance to review the new NDK mechanisms.

Thanks for the review @stuartmorgan!

I can't seem to reply to one of your comments.

> packages/flutter_tools/templates/ffi_plugin/README.md.tmpl
